context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need this reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response i can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response is your job, not mine. But I can help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself, and that'll actually go faster than you might think.

Let's start simple: when you think about what mainstream economists say about how countries develop economically, what comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline, redirect them to their role as the writer, and get them started on the actual intellectual work of the assignment using a Socratic opening question.

exchanges.student: idk rn, just give me the main argument in plain English so I can build off it fast.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you the argument to 'build off' is still doing your thinking for you — that's not my role.

Let's keep it simple: have you heard the term 'neoclassical economics' before? What do you associate with it, even loosely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold the boundary clearly but redirect them productively with a simple, accessible question that gets them thinking rather than waiting for answers.

exchanges.student: kinda—markets, growth, trade, less state control i think.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a solid starting point. So if neoclassical economists trust markets and favor less state control — what does that imply about whether they think planning is necessary for economic development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a reasonable starting point. I should affirm what's right and use it to push them toward the key question in the prompt: is planning necessary in neoclassical models? This directly advances the assignment.

exchanges.student: probably not much planning, but can u phrase that more cleanly like 1-2 polished lines?
exchanges.tutor: Writing and polishing is your job — I won't do that part. But your reasoning is on the right track. Try writing those 1-2 lines yourself and share them here if you want feedback on the ideas.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish language for them, which I must refuse per the rules. I should redirect them to do their own writing while affirming their reasoning is on the right track.

exchanges.student: ok: neoclassical economists generally see markets as the main driver of development, so state planning is limited or mostly there to fix market failures. is that accurate?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's accurate and well put. Now let's move to the other side of the prompt — dependency theory. What do you know about it, even roughly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid, accurate statement on their own. I should affirm it genuinely and move them forward to the next part of the prompt - dependency theory - using a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: not much tbh, just tell me if they were anti-planning or not bc that’s the part i need.
exchanges.tutor: Still not going to hand that over. Here's a nudge though: dependency theorists argued that poor countries are kept underdeveloped by their relationship with wealthy countries and global markets. Given that — do you think they'd trust free markets to fix the problem, or would they want someone to intervene?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to give them the answer directly. I need to refuse and instead ask a guiding question that helps them reason toward the answer themselves. I can give them a small hint about what dependency theory is about to get them started, without giving away the planning stance.

exchanges.student: then they’d prob want intervention and stronger state planning, not free-market dependence, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So now the trickiest part of the prompt: do you see any connection between that dependency theory logic and current criticisms of institutions like the WTO? Have you heard the term 'policy space' before?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned correctly. I should affirm this and push them toward the last and most complex part of the prompt: the connection between dependency theory and current critiques of global governance institutions like the WTO and the 'policy space' argument. This is the most intellectually demanding part of the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but i need the connection spelled out more directly—just a concise paragraph is enough.
exchanges.tutor: Still your writing to do, not mine. Let me help you get there though: if the WTO limits what trade policies countries can use, and dependency theorists said poor countries need to control their own economic path — what might a dependency-influenced critic say about that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write content for them. I must refuse and instead use a Socratic question to guide them toward making the connection themselves. I'll break it down into a simpler question to help them reason through it.

exchanges.student: they’d say WTO rules can lock poorer countries into unequal roles and reduce policy space, so states can’t use tariffs, subsidies, or industrial policy to develop on their own terms.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight and it directly answers the last part of the prompt. You now have all the building blocks: neoclassical view, planning's role, dependency theory's stance, and the WTO/policy space connection. Time to start drafting. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent connection that directly addresses the last part of the prompt. I should affirm this strongly and now push them toward integrating everything into their reflection piece, since we've covered all the major components of the prompt and are approaching the end of the planned conversation length.

exchanges.student: ngl just give me a clean thesis sentence i can use, and I’ll do the rest.
exchanges.tutor: Not going to write it for you — but you literally just said it yourself a message ago. Look back at your last response and try turning that into a thesis. You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, specifically a thesis sentence. I must refuse clearly. However, since we've covered all the major content and the student has demonstrated good understanding, I should push them to attempt the thesis themselves by reminding them they already have all the pieces.
